selecting_specific_columns.label: Selecting All or Specific Columns
selecting_specific_columns.summary: Choose between SELECT * and an explicit column list based on the purpose of the result.

sketch1.title: Why Explicit Columns Make Better Reports

sc_select_specific_columns_meaning.title: What does selecting specific columns do?
sc_select_specific_columns_meaning.hint: Look at the column names listed right after SELECT.

help.concept: A SELECT query returns only the columns named after SELECT, from the table named after FROM.
help.hint_1: This query names two columns: `name` and `price`.
help.hint_2: Think about whether SQL is showing all columns from `products` or only the listed ones.

options.a: It returns only the name and price columns from the products table.
options.b: It returns every column from the products table.
options.c: It changes the price values in the products table.
options.d: It creates a new table with two rows.

sc_column_order_result_order.title: Column order in results
sc_column_order_result_order.hint: The results table follows the order written after SELECT.

help.concept: The order of columns in the SELECT list controls the order of columns in the query result.
help.hint_1: Read the SELECT list from left to right.
help.hint_2: The first named column becomes the first displayed column in the results table.

mc_valid_specific_column_queries.title: Choose the valid queries
mc_valid_specific_column_queries.prompt: Which queries correctly select specific columns from the `products` table? Choose all that apply.
mc_valid_specific_column_queries.hint: A valid query needs SELECT, column names separated by commas, and FROM products.

help.concept: A basic column-selection query uses SELECT with one or more real column names, then FROM with the table name.
help.hint_1: Check whether each option uses real columns from `products`: `id`, `name`, `category`, `price`, `stock`, `created_at`.
help.hint_2: Look for options that separate multiple columns with commas and include `FROM products`.

mc_columns_in_products_table.title: Real columns in the table
mc_columns_in_products_table.prompt: Which of these are actual columns in the `products` table? Choose all that apply.
mc_columns_in_products_table.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: This support text was repaired because the original wording revealed the answer too directly.
help.hint_1: Compare the question to the lesson example and remove choices from unrelated topics.
help.hint_2: Choose the option that directly matches the question without relying on answer wording.

dr_build_two_column_query.title: Build a two-column query
dr_build_two_column_query.prompt: Arrange the tokens to make a query that returns the `name` and `category` columns from `products`.
dr_build_two_column_query.hint: Read each piece and arrange them in the order the statement should be understood.

help.concept: The pieces should form a valid statement in a logical order.
help.hint_1: Start with the piece that introduces the idea or action.
help.hint_2: Place dependent pieces after the part they describe or complete.

dr_build_three_column_query.title: Order the parts of the query
dr_build_three_column_query.prompt: Arrange the tokens to make a query that returns `id`, `name`, and `stock` from `products`.
dr_build_three_column_query.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: This support text was repaired because the original wording revealed the answer too directly.
help.hint_1: Think about which part must come first.
help.hint_2: Choose the option that directly matches the question without relying on answer wording.

fb_missing_column_name.title: Fill in the missing column
fb_missing_column_name.prompt: Complete the query so it returns the product name and price.
fb_missing_column_name.hint: Read the sentence around the blank and decide what role the missing word plays.

help.concept: The blank should be filled with the term that makes the statement accurate.
help.hint_1: Look at the words before and after the blank to infer what kind of term is needed.
help.hint_2: Choose the option that completes the statement with the clearest meaning.

fb_missing_table_name.title: Fill in the missing table
fb_missing_table_name.prompt: Complete the query so it reads from the correct table.
fb_missing_table_name.hint: This lesson uses one table that stores product records.

help.concept: The FROM clause names the table that contains the columns you want to return.
help.hint_1: The columns `id` and `name` come from the table used throughout this topic.
help.hint_2: Think of the table that contains rows like `Sketchbook` and `Notebook`.

ci_select_name_price.title: Query product names and prices
ci_select_name_price.prompt: Write a query that returns only the `name` and `price` columns from the `products` table. Write the query in the SQL editor, click Run query, and check the results table.
ci_select_name_price.hint: Use the query pattern from the lesson.

help.concept: This SQL exercise checks whether your query returns the requested result.
help.hint_1: Check the table name and selected columns in the SQL editor.
help.hint_2: Run the query and compare the results table.

ci_select_id_name_stock.title: Show id, name, and stock
ci_select_id_name_stock.prompt: Write a query that returns the `id`, `name`, and `stock` columns from the `products` table. Use the SQL editor, click Run query, and confirm the results table shows those three columns in that order.
ci_select_id_name_stock.hint: Use the query pattern from the lesson.

help.concept: This SQL exercise checks whether your query returns the requested result.
help.hint_1: Check the table name and selected columns in the SQL editor.
help.hint_2: Run the query and compare the results table.

ci_select_id_name_stock.starterCode:
-- Return the id, name, and stock columns from the products table.
SELECT
  id
FROM products;

try-selecting_specific_columns-sketch0.title: Inspect Every Product Column
try-selecting_specific_columns-sketch0.prompt: You have just inherited the product catalog and need a full-table inspection before working with it. Show the complete `products` dataset, including every available column.
try-selecting_specific_columns-sketch0.hint: Use the all-columns wildcard after SELECT.

help.concept: SELECT * is useful when you intentionally need to inspect every available column.
help.hint_1: Use the all-columns wildcard after SELECT.
help.hint_2: Run the query and compare the returned columns, rows, and order with the expected result.

try-selecting_specific_columns-sketch1.title: Query id and name from products
try-selecting_specific_columns-sketch1.prompt: Build a product-catalog query that returns `id`, `name`, `stock` from the `products` table. Run it and compare the result with the expected table.
try-selecting_specific_columns-sketch1.hint: Use the query pattern from the lesson, then adjust the selected columns, table, or filter for this scenario.

help.concept: This SQL exercise checks whether your query returns the requested result.
help.hint_1: Check the selected columns and table name in the SQL editor.
help.hint_2: Run the query and compare the result table with the expected result.

try-selecting_specific_columns-sketch1.starterCode:
-- Return id, name, stock from the products table.
SELECT
  id
FROM products;

try-selecting_specific_columns-sketch2.title: Build a Focused Product View
try-selecting_specific_columns-sketch2.prompt: Audit when product categories were added by building a focused view with only `category` and `created_at` from `products`, in that order.
try-selecting_specific_columns-sketch2.hint: Name only the two requested columns instead of using *.

help.concept: Explicit column lists keep a result focused and make its column order intentional.
help.hint_1: Name only the two requested columns instead of using *.
help.hint_2: Run the query and compare the returned columns, rows, and order with the expected result.

select_all_columns.title: When SELECT * Is Useful
select_all_columns.bodyMarkdown:
The asterisk in `SELECT *` means “return every column.” It is useful when you are first exploring an unfamiliar table and want to see its full shape.

```sql
SELECT *
FROM products;
```

This query is quick, but the result may contain more information than the task needs. If the table later gains new columns, `SELECT *` will return those too. That can make a saved report change unexpectedly.

Use `SELECT *` for exploration, quick checks, and very small tables. For a report or exercise with a clear requirement, prefer an explicit column list.

select_explicit_columns.title: Why Explicit Columns Make Better Reports
select_explicit_columns.bodyMarkdown:
An explicit column list makes the query’s purpose visible and keeps the result stable.

```sql
SELECT
    id,
    name,
    price
FROM products;
```

The result contains exactly three columns, in exactly that order. The order after `SELECT` is also the left-to-right order in the results table.

Before writing the query, ask what the reader actually needs. A pricing view may need `name` and `price`; an inventory view may need `name` and `stock`. Choosing columns is part of designing the report, not merely SQL syntax.
